content.text: Tracking model versions on a technology radar is a discipline that sits at the intersection of dependency management, performance benchmarking, security operations, and compliance governance. Unlike traditional library dependencies — which follow semantic versioning and can be pinned to a stable release — AI model versions are identified by opaque snapshot dates, change provider-side without client-side action, and can alter system behaviour in ways that only become visible through prompt regression testing. This sub-subtopic covers the lifecycle of a model version from experimental preview through production stability to deprecation and retirement, the YAML entry format for capturing model version state on the radar, and the Python tooling for tracking the full lifecycle automatically.

The scope includes both frontier API models (accessed via provider APIs such as Anthropic's and OpenAI's) and self-hosted open-source models (Llama, Mistral, Qwen) where the organisation controls the deployment. SDK versions — the client libraries used to call the models — are a parallel tracking concern with their own lifecycle; they are covered in the second half of this sub-subtopic.

attrs.alt: Model version lifecycle diagram showing four states (Experimental, Production, Deprecated, Retired) with corresponding radar rings and SDK version tracking row
attrs.title: Model Version Lifecycle and Radar Tracking

content.text: The fundamental difficulty in tracking AI model versions is that the relationship between a model identifier and the model's behaviour is
in ways that are unlike software packages. When you install
from PyPI, you receive exactly the same code as anyone else who installs the same version, and the code will not change after installation. When you call
via an API, you are calling a version that is pinned by the
, but the inference infrastructure, RLHF layer, and safety filters may be updated without changing the identifier.
This creates several tracking challenges that are not present in traditional software dependency management:

Providers may update the inference infrastructure (batching, quantisation, safety layer) without changing the model snapshot identifier. The API name you track in the radar may correspond to subtly different behaviour over time. The only way to detect this is continuous prompt regression testing.

Providers release model families (Claude 3.5, GPT-4o) with multiple versioned instances (claude-3-5-sonnet-20240620, claude-3-5-sonnet-20241022). The family name in documentation may refer to any version in the family. Tracking at the family level loses the specificity needed for reproducibility and security review.

Cloud API models are typically deprecated 6–18 months after release. Open-source model weights are never deprecated (you control them), but the supporting inference infrastructure (vLLM, Ollama versions that optimise for them) may stop being maintained. The organisation's deprecation response window is often shorter than the notice period.

Prompts tuned for claude-3-sonnet may perform differently on claude-3.5-sonnet, not because the newer model is worse, but because its instruction-following characteristics changed. This creates a testing requirement every time a model is upgraded.
The client SDK version couples to the model API version in non-obvious ways. An upgrade from
may be required to access new model features, but the upgrade may itself introduce breaking changes in the SDK's Python interface.
Every model version tracked on the radar passes through four lifecycle states. The state determines the radar ring, the governance requirements, and the actions the organisation must take.

A model is in the Experimental state from the moment the provider grants API access (preview, beta, or early access) until the model is declared Generally Available with a stable version identifier. In the radar, Experimental models are placed in the Assess ring.

During the Experimental phase, the model's behaviour, token limits, rate limits, and pricing are subject to change without notice. The provider may retract early access. Safety properties are being evaluated and may change. For these reasons, Experimental models must not be used in production or in systems that process sensitive data. Permitted uses are isolated sandbox evaluations, benchmark comparisons, and internal demos with synthetic data.
The key action during the Experimental phase is to
— the prompt regression test suite — that will be used to assess the model when it moves to Production. This ensures the team is ready to complete the Production assessment quickly after GA.

A model enters the Production state when the provider declares General Availability with a stable, versioned model identifier that is committed to be maintained without behavioural changes for a defined support period. In the radar, Production models are placed in Trial (newly promoted) or Adopt (after a successful trial period).
The governance requirements for promoting a model to Production state on the radar are:
For API models, the specific dated version identifier (e.g.,
) must be pinned in all code that calls the model. Never use a floating alias (e.g.,
) in production; a floating alias may silently move to a different model version.

A model enters the Deprecated state when the provider announces a future retirement date. In the radar, Deprecated models are moved to Hold. No new deployments of a Deprecated model are permitted, but existing systems that use it may continue operating until the retirement date — provided the organisation has an active migration plan with a target completion date before the retirement date.

Deprecation notices typically arrive 6–12 months before the retirement date for major providers. The governance response must happen within 2 weeks of the notice: create a migration issue, assign an owner, identify the target replacement model, and document the migration effort estimate.

The Hold entry in the radar for a Deprecated model should include the retirement date, the replacement model recommendation, and the migration status. This is visible to all teams at the next radar review, creating accountability for the migration.

A model is Retired when the provider's API returns an error for that model identifier (typically HTTP 404 or 410). Any system that has not completed migration by this date is broken. In the radar, Retired models are archived rather than removed: the entry is marked as retired with the retirement date, and the historical performance data and incident log are preserved for audit purposes.

If the organisation has systems that are still using a Retired model at retirement date, this is a production incident. The rollback plan documented during the Production governance review becomes the immediate response playbook.
YAML Model Version Radar Entry Format

The YAML format for model versions extends the standard radar entry format with model-specific fields: the exact versioned identifier, the lifecycle state, the support window, prompt regression test results, and the migration path when deprecated.

# model-version-radar-entries.yaml
# Subject: agentic-ai
# Maintained by: ai-platform-team
# Format version: 2.0

model_entries:

  - id: claude-3-7-sonnet-20250219
    name: "Claude 3.7 Sonnet (2025-02-19)"
    provider: anthropic
    quadrant: llm_models
    ring: adopt
    lifecycle_state: production
    model_family: claude-3-7
    api_identifier: "claude-3-7-sonnet-20250219"  # exact string to pin in code
    floating_alias: "claude-3-7-sonnet-latest"     # never use in production

    support_window:
      ga_date: "2025-02-19"
      deprecation_notice_date: null    # not yet announced
      retirement_date: null
      expected_support_months: 18      # Anthropic's typical support window

    performance:
      internal_benchmark_score: 91     # percentile on internal task suite
      vs_previous_model: "+12%"        # vs claude-3-5-sonnet-20241022
      p50_latency_ms: 820
      p99_latency_ms: 3200
      cost_per_1m_input_tokens_usd: 3.0
      cost_per_1m_output_tokens_usd: 15.0

    prompt_regression:
      last_run_date: "2026-06-01"
      test_count: 847
      pass_rate: 0.976
      failures: 21
      known_behavioural_differences_from_previous:
        - "Longer default preambles in multi-turn without explicit brevity instruction"
        - "Improved refusal consistency on edge-case safety inputs"

    security_review:
      completed_date: "2025-03-15"
      reviewer: "security-team"
      injection_resistance_score: 8.5
      data_residency_regions: [us-east-1, eu-west-1]
      soc2_type2: true
      findings: []
      next_review_date: "2026-03-15"

    compliance:
      gdpr_dpa_signed: true
      hipaa_baa_available: true
      pci_dss_in_scope: false

    sdk_requirements:
      minimum_anthropic_sdk: "0.27.0"

    migration:
      replaces: "claude-3-5-sonnet-20241022"
      migration_effort_days: 3
      migration_notes: "Prompt format compatible. Update model ID string and run regression suite."

    owner: ai-platform-team
    last_reviewed: "2026-07-01"
    next_review_date: "2026-10-01"

  - id: claude-3-5-sonnet-20241022
    name: "Claude 3.5 Sonnet (2024-10-22)"
    provider: anthropic
    quadrant: llm_models
    ring: hold
    lifecycle_state: deprecated
    model_family: claude-3-5

    api_identifier: "claude-3-5-sonnet-20241022"

    support_window:
      ga_date: "2024-10-22"
      deprecation_notice_date: "2026-05-01"
      retirement_date: "2026-11-01"
      expected_support_months: 24

    hold_reason: >
      Deprecation announced 2026-05-01 with retirement on 2026-11-01.
      No new deployments permitted. All teams must migrate to
      claude-3-7-sonnet-20250219 by 2026-10-15.

    migration:
      target_model: "claude-3-7-sonnet-20250219"
      migration_deadline: "2026-10-15"
      migration_status: "in_progress"
      teams_migrated: ["customer-support-agent", "doc-processing-pipeline"]
      teams_pending: ["internal-search-agent", "report-generator"]
      migration_owner: ai-platform-team

    owner: ai-platform-team
    last_reviewed: "2026-07-01"
    next_review_date: "2026-08-01"   # monthly review during deprecation window

  - id: llama-3-3-70b-instruct
    name: "Llama 3.3 70B Instruct (self-hosted)"
    provider: meta-ai
    quadrant: llm_models
    ring: trial
    lifecycle_state: production
    model_family: llama-3-3

    api_identifier: "meta-llama/Llama-3.3-70B-Instruct"
    weights_source: "https://huggingface.co/meta-llama/Llama-3.3-70B-Instruct"
    weights_sha256: "a1b2c3d4..."   # verify on download

    support_window:
      weights_published_date: "2024-12-06"
      provider_support_commitment: "community"  # no SLA
      retirement_date: null

    infrastructure:
      serving_framework: "vllm==0.6.4"
      hardware: "4x A100 80GB"
      deployment: "internal-gpu-cluster"

    performance:
      internal_benchmark_score: 78
      vs_current_adopt: "-13%"   # vs claude-3-7-sonnet on internal suite
      p50_latency_ms: 1100
      cost_per_1k_tokens_usd: 0.0008   # compute cost only

    prompt_regression:
      last_run_date: "2026-06-15"
      test_count: 847
      pass_rate: 0.904

    security_review:
      completed_date: "2026-05-20"
      weights_integrity_verified: true
      backdoor_scan: "passed"     # ran lm-evaluation-harness safety probes
      data_residency: "internal-only"   # no data leaves org

    trial_scope: "internal document processing — no customer-facing or PII use"
    trial_end_date: "2026-09-30"
    trial_success_criteria: "pass_rate >= 0.93 on extended benchmark"

    owner: ai-platform-team

The lifecycle tracker automates the operational burden of model version management: monitoring for retirement deadlines, alerting on overdue migrations, running the prompt regression test suite on a schedule, and generating a lifecycle status report for the radar review meeting.

# model_lifecycle_tracker.py
# Automated model version lifecycle tracking for the agentic AI radar
import json
import hashlib
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from datetime import date, timedelta
from typing import Optional
import yaml

@dataclass
class MigrationStatus:
    target_model: str
    deadline: date
    days_until_deadline: int
    status: str
    teams_pending: list[str]
    is_overdue: bool

@dataclass
class ModelLifecycleReport:
    model_id: str
    lifecycle_state: str
    radar_ring: str
    days_until_retirement: Optional[int]
    migration: Optional[MigrationStatus]
    regression_pass_rate: Optional[float]
    last_security_review_date: Optional[date]
    days_since_security_review: Optional[int]
    alerts: list[str]

class ModelLifecycleTracker:
    SECURITY_REVIEW_MAX_DAYS = 365
    REGRESSION_TEST_MIN_PASS_RATE = 0.95
    DEPRECATION_ALERT_DAYS = [90, 60, 30, 14, 7]

    def __init__(self, radar_path: str):
        with open(radar_path) as f:
            data = yaml.safe_load(f)
        self._entries = {e["id"]: e for e in data.get("model_entries", [])}

    def generate_report(self, model_id: str, as_of: date = None) -> ModelLifecycleReport:
        if as_of is None:
            as_of = date.today()

        entry = self._entries.get(model_id)
        if not entry:
            raise KeyError(f"Model '{model_id}' not found in radar")

        alerts = []
        sw = entry.get("support_window", {})
        retirement_date = None
        days_until_retirement = None

        if sw.get("retirement_date"):
            retirement_date = date.fromisoformat(sw["retirement_date"])
            days_until_retirement = (retirement_date - as_of).days
            for threshold in self.DEPRECATION_ALERT_DAYS:
                if 0 <= days_until_retirement <= threshold:
                    alerts.append(
                        f"RETIREMENT IN {days_until_retirement} DAYS "
                        f"(deadline: {retirement_date})"
                    )
                    break
            if days_until_retirement < 0:
                alerts.append(f"RETIREMENT DATE PASSED {abs(days_until_retirement)} DAYS AGO — OUTAGE RISK")

        migration_status = None
        mig = entry.get("migration", {})
        if entry.get("lifecycle_state") == "deprecated" and mig.get("migration_deadline"):
            deadline = date.fromisoformat(mig["migration_deadline"])
            days_remaining = (deadline - as_of).days
            pending_teams = mig.get("teams_pending", [])
            migration_status = MigrationStatus(
                target_model=mig.get("target_model", "unknown"),
                deadline=deadline,
                days_until_deadline=days_remaining,
                status=mig.get("migration_status", "unknown"),
                teams_pending=pending_teams,
                is_overdue=days_remaining < 0,
            )
            if pending_teams and days_remaining <= 30:
                alerts.append(
                    f"MIGRATION DEADLINE IN {days_remaining}d — "
                    f"{len(pending_teams)} team(s) pending: {', '.join(pending_teams)}"
                )
            if migration_status.is_overdue and pending_teams:
                alerts.append(
                    f"MIGRATION OVERDUE — {len(pending_teams)} team(s) still on deprecated model"
                )

        pr = entry.get("prompt_regression", {})
        regression_pass_rate = pr.get("pass_rate")
        if regression_pass_rate is not None and regression_pass_rate < self.REGRESSION_TEST_MIN_PASS_RATE:
            alerts.append(
                f"REGRESSION PASS RATE {regression_pass_rate:.1%} BELOW THRESHOLD "
                f"{self.REGRESSION_TEST_MIN_PASS_RATE:.1%}"
            )
        last_run = pr.get("last_run_date")
        if last_run:
            days_since_regression = (as_of - date.fromisoformat(last_run)).days
            if days_since_regression > 30 and entry.get("lifecycle_state") == "production":
                alerts.append(f"REGRESSION TESTS LAST RUN {days_since_regression} DAYS AGO — run monthly in production")

        sec = entry.get("security_review", {})
        sec_review_date = None
        days_since_review = None
        if sec.get("completed_date"):
            sec_review_date = date.fromisoformat(sec["completed_date"])
            days_since_review = (as_of - sec_review_date).days
            if days_since_review > self.SECURITY_REVIEW_MAX_DAYS:
                alerts.append(
                    f"SECURITY REVIEW OVERDUE — last reviewed {days_since_review} days ago "
                    f"(max: {self.SECURITY_REVIEW_MAX_DAYS} days)"
                )

        return ModelLifecycleReport(
            model_id=model_id,
            lifecycle_state=entry.get("lifecycle_state", "unknown"),
            radar_ring=entry.get("ring", "unknown"),
            days_until_retirement=days_until_retirement,
            migration=migration_status,
            regression_pass_rate=regression_pass_rate,
            last_security_review_date=sec_review_date,
            days_since_security_review=days_since_review,
            alerts=alerts,
        )

    def generate_all_reports(self, as_of: date = None) -> list[ModelLifecycleReport]:
        reports = [self.generate_report(mid, as_of) for mid in self._entries]
        return sorted(reports, key=lambda r: len(r.alerts), reverse=True)

    def print_dashboard(self, as_of: date = None) -> None:
        reports = self.generate_all_reports(as_of)
        print(f"Model Version Lifecycle Dashboard — {as_of or date.today()}")
        print("=" * 70)
        for r in reports:
            status_icon = "OK" if not r.alerts else "!!"
            retire_str = f"retires in {r.days_until_retirement}d" if r.days_until_retirement else "no EOL"
            pass_str = f"{r.regression_pass_rate:.1%}" if r.regression_pass_rate else "N/A"
            print(f"[{status_icon}] {r.model_id:<45} {r.radar_ring:<8} pass={pass_str} {retire_str}")
            for alert in r.alerts:
                print(f"     ALERT: {alert}")
        print()


# Prompt regression test runner
class PromptRegressionRunner:
    def __init__(self, model_client, test_suite_path: str):
        self._client = model_client
        with open(test_suite_path) as f:
            self._tests = json.load(f)

    def run(self, model_id: str) -> dict:
        results = {"passed": 0, "failed": 0, "failures": []}
        for test in self._tests:
            response = self._client.messages.create(
                model=model_id,
                max_tokens=test.get("max_tokens", 512),
                messages=[{"role": "user", "content": test["prompt"]}],
            )
            actual = response.content[0].text.strip()
            expected_hash = test.get("expected_output_hash")
            actual_hash = hashlib.sha256(actual.encode()).hexdigest()
            if test.get("exact_match"):
                passed = actual == test["expected_output"]
            elif expected_hash:
                passed = actual_hash == expected_hash
            else:
                # Semantic check: verify all required phrases appear
                required = test.get("required_phrases", [])
                forbidden = test.get("forbidden_phrases", [])
                passed = (
                    all(phrase.lower() in actual.lower() for phrase in required) and
                    not any(phrase.lower() in actual.lower() for phrase in forbidden)
                )

            if passed:
                results["passed"] += 1
            else:
                results["failed"] += 1
                results["failures"].append({
                    "test_id": test["id"],
                    "prompt": test["prompt"][:80],
                    "actual_hash": actual_hash,
                })

        total = results["passed"] + results["failed"]
        results["pass_rate"] = results["passed"] / total if total > 0 else 0.0
        results["model_id"] = model_id
        results["test_count"] = total
        return results

SDK versions — the Python, TypeScript, or other language packages used to call model APIs — are a distinct tracking concern from model versions. An SDK upgrade can break production code independently of any model change, and a model version may require a minimum SDK version to access new features.

The radar tracks SDK versions in the same quadrant as the models they support. The key difference is that SDK versions follow semantic versioning, so the tracking model is closer to traditional software dependency management. The lifecycle states (Experimental, Production, Deprecated, Retired) still apply, but the deprecation signals come from the package repository and release notes rather than from a provider's API policy.
API model features and SDK versions are coupled in both directions:
. Organisations that have pinned an older SDK version cannot use the feature without an SDK upgrade, which may itself require code changes.
Model API changes requiring SDK updates:

When Anthropic changed the messages API schema in the v1 to v2 migration, the old SDK version sent requests in a format the new API did not accept. Organisations that did not track the SDK-to-API coupling missed the migration window.
SDK deprecation ahead of model deprecation:
Older SDK major versions may be deprecated before the models they support. An organisation on
(not yet retired) but facing a broken SDK that no longer receives security patches.

# sdk-version-radar-entries.yaml
sdk_entries:

  - id: anthropic-sdk-python-0.27
    name: "anthropic Python SDK 0.27.x"
    quadrant: llm_models
    ring: adopt
    lifecycle_state: production
    package: anthropic
    version_range: ">=0.27.0,<0.28.0"
    pypi_url: "https://pypi.org/project/anthropic/"

    supports_models:
      - claude-3-7-sonnet-20250219
      - claude-3-5-sonnet-20241022
      - claude-3-5-haiku-20241022

    breaking_changes_from_previous:
      - "streaming: stream() context manager replaces raw_stream()"
      - "tool use: ToolUseBlock type moved to anthropic.types"

    security:
      known_cves: []
      last_audit_date: "2026-05-01"

    owner: ai-platform-team

  - id: anthropic-sdk-python-0.2
    name: "anthropic Python SDK 0.2.x"
    quadrant: llm_models
    ring: hold
    lifecycle_state: deprecated
    package: anthropic
    version_range: "0.2.*"

    hold_reason: >
      SDK 0.2.x reached end-of-life in 2024. Does not support models
      released after claude-2.0. No security patches. All teams must
      migrate to 0.27.x.

    migration:
      target_sdk: "anthropic>=0.27.0"
      migration_deadline: "2026-09-01"
      breaking_changes:
        - "Messages API replaces Completions API"
        - "Client class renamed: Anthropic() replaces Client()"
        - "All response types changed"

    owner: ai-platform-team

Model updates in a production agentic system are changes with the potential to alter system behaviour at scale. The governance process must be proportional to the risk: a routine model version upgrade on an internal tool requires less rigour than upgrading the model in a customer-facing financial agent.
Customer-facing, financial impact, PII processing, regulated
Security review + prompt regression + performance benchmark + load test + staged rollout + rollback plan
Internal, significant business process, non-PII sensitive data
Prompt regression + performance benchmark + rollback plan
Never upgrade a model in production in a single cut-over for Tier 1 or Tier 2 systems. Use a staged rollout that progressively routes traffic to the new model while monitoring for regressions:

Implement the traffic split at the agent runner level, not at the API level. The agent runner selects the model based on a feature flag controlled by the deployment system. This allows instant rollback by flipping the flag rather than deploying new code.

# model_router.py
# Traffic-splitting model router for staged model rollouts
import random
import logging
from dataclasses import dataclass

logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)

@dataclass
class ModelRouteConfig:
    primary_model: str       # current production model
    candidate_model: str     # model being rolled out
    candidate_pct: float     # fraction of traffic to candidate (0.0 - 1.0)
    shadow_only: bool = False  # if True, candidate runs but its output is not used

class ModelRouter:
    def __init__(self, config: ModelRouteConfig, metrics_sink=None):
        self._config = config
        self._metrics = metrics_sink

    def select_model(self, session_id: str) -> tuple[str, bool]:
        # Deterministic per session: same session always gets same model
        # Avoids mid-session model switches that could break conversation consistency
        session_hash = hash(session_id) % 100
        use_candidate = session_hash < int(self._config.candidate_pct * 100)
        model = self._config.candidate_model if use_candidate else self._config.primary_model
        is_shadow = use_candidate and self._config.shadow_only

        logger.info("ModelRouter: session=%s model=%s shadow=%s", session_id, model, is_shadow)
        if self._metrics:
            self._metrics.increment("model_router.selection", tags={"model": model, "shadow": str(is_shadow)})

        return model, is_shadow

    def update_candidate_pct(self, new_pct: float) -> None:
        self._config.candidate_pct = new_pct
        logger.info("ModelRouter: candidate_pct updated to %.0f%%", new_pct * 100)


# Integration with agent runner
class StagedRolloutAgentRunner:
    def __init__(self, agent_factory, router: ModelRouter):
        self._agent_factory = agent_factory
        self._router = router

    def run(self, task: str, session_id: str) -> str:
        model, is_shadow = self._router.select_model(session_id)
        agent = self._agent_factory(model=model)
        result = agent.run(task)

        if is_shadow:
            # Shadow mode: log result but return None to signal "use primary"
            logger.info("ShadowResult[%s]: %s", model, result[:120])
            return None  # caller uses primary result
        return result

# Configuration examples for each rollout stage
STAGE_SHADOW = ModelRouteConfig(
    primary_model="claude-3-5-sonnet-20241022",
    candidate_model="claude-3-7-sonnet-20250219",
    candidate_pct=0.05,
    shadow_only=True,
)
STAGE_CANARY = ModelRouteConfig(
    primary_model="claude-3-5-sonnet-20241022",
    candidate_model="claude-3-7-sonnet-20250219",
    candidate_pct=0.05,
    shadow_only=False,
)
STAGE_FULL = ModelRouteConfig(
    primary_model="claude-3-7-sonnet-20250219",
    candidate_model="claude-3-7-sonnet-20250219",
    candidate_pct=1.0,
    shadow_only=False,
)
When to Apply Model Version Tracking

Model version tracking applies to any system that calls an AI model API. Even a simple one-model system benefits from the governance structure because models are deprecated and the organisation needs advance warning to execute migrations. The level of tracking investment should be proportional to the system's risk tier:
Pin the model ID to an exact versioned identifier in code. Never use floating aliases in production. Create a YAML radar entry when the model first enters Trial.
Implement prompt regression test suite. Run it before every model upgrade and on a monthly schedule to detect behaviour drift. Monitor regression pass rate in the radar entry.

Implement staged rollout. Set up automated lifecycle alert monitoring (deprecation deadlines, security review expiry). Integrate the lifecycle tracker into the CI/CD pipeline to block deployments that reference Hold-ring models.
Real-World Example: Managing the claude-3.5 to claude-3.7 Migration
A team running a customer-facing legal document review agent on
receives a deprecation notice with an 8-month retirement window. The governance process runs as follows:
Week 1: The deprecation notice triggers automatic creation of a Hold entry for
in the radar YAML. The lifecycle tracker generates an alert. The ai-platform team opens a migration issue and assigns it to the agent team. The target model —
— is already in Adopt ring with a completed security review, so no new security review is required.

Weeks 2–3: The agent team runs the prompt regression suite (847 tests) against the new model. Pass rate: 97.6% vs 97.1% on the old model. The 21 failures are reviewed manually: 14 are formatting differences that are not customer-visible, 5 are more verbose outputs that require adjusting output post-processing, and 2 are genuine capability differences that require prompt updates. The prompt updates are implemented and the suite is re-run with 98.1% pass rate.

Weeks 4–7: Shadow mode deployment. 5% of production traffic runs through the new model in parallel. No customer impact. The team reviews 200 shadowed outputs manually, sampling equally across document types. No concerning outputs detected.

Weeks 8–9: Canary at 5%. Error rate stable. Latency P99 slightly higher (+18%) due to the new model's extended reasoning. The team adjusts timeout settings and re-runs load tests. Canary expanded to 20%.
Week 10: Full cutover to the new model. The radar entry for
remains at Hold with migration status 'completed' until retirement date, at which point it moves to Retired. The lifecycle tracker auto-closes the migration alert.
Using model family names as API identifiers:
in production means the provider may silently reroute your requests to a different snapshot when they update the alias. Pin the dated identifier.
Running regression tests only at upgrade time:
Models can drift behaviourally even when the version identifier is pinned, because the inference infrastructure changes. Run regression tests monthly in production to detect drift before users do.

Provider deprecation notices are published in release notes, email announcements, and API documentation updates — not all in the same channel. Set up multiple monitoring signals: provider status page watchers, email digest subscriptions, and the lifecycle tracker's automated polling of provider APIs.

Teams document the rollback plan but never verify it works. During the shadow phase, execute a full rollback drill: flip the feature flag back to the old model, verify traffic returns to the primary, and confirm no session state was corrupted.
Treating SDK upgrade and model upgrade as the same event:
from 0.23 to 0.27 while simultaneously switching from
combines two sources of change and makes it impossible to isolate which change caused a regression. Sequence SDK upgrades separately from model upgrades.

The lifecycle tracker should poll provider documentation APIs or parse provider RSS/email feeds to create Deprecated entries automatically when providers announce retirement dates. The human radar review should confirm the entry and assign the migration owner, not discover the deprecation.
Maintain a regression test suite as a first-class artefact:

The regression test suite is as important as production code. Store it in version control, review it when the model changes (some tests may need to be updated for new model capabilities), and run it in CI on every pull request that touches model configuration.
Include model version in all audit logs:

Every agent session log should include the exact model version ID used for inference. This makes it possible to correlate an incident with the specific model version that was running at the time, even if the model has since been upgraded.
Set migration deadline before provider retirement date:
Internal migration deadlines should be 4–6 weeks before the provider's retirement date for Tier 1 systems. This provides a buffer for unexpected migration complications without creating an emergency.
Track behaviour delta, not just pass rate:

When the regression suite pass rate is the same on both models, the models are not necessarily behaving identically. Review the actual outputs of the failed tests and sample the passed tests to understand how the new model's responses differ in character, not just in correctness.
Performance and Scalability of the Tracking System

The lifecycle tracker and regression runner are operational tools, not production-critical paths. They run asynchronously and their performance requirements are modest: the dashboard should generate in under 5 seconds; the full regression suite should complete in under 30 minutes for a 1000-test suite at typical model API latency.

For large organisations with many deployed models and agents, the tracker can be run as a scheduled job (hourly for deadline alerts, daily for dashboard generation, monthly for regression runs) and its output published to a shared dashboard. The YAML radar entries should be stored in a centralised repository with webhook-triggered updates when entries change.

MLOps platform that supports model version tracking, prompt regression logging, and performance comparison across model versions. Integrates with the regression runner to store per-version test results.
Open-source platform for registering, versioning, and staging ML models. Can be adapted for tracking API model versions by using the model URI as a pointer to the provider's API identifier.

Automated dependency update tools that open PRs when new SDK versions are published. Configure to alert on (but not auto-merge) SDK updates for AI packages, since these require manual regression testing.
Anthropic publishes model deprecation timelines at docs.anthropic.com. The lifecycle tracker can poll the published deprecation table programmatically.
Similar to Anthropic — a published deprecation schedule for all OpenAI API models. The lifecycle tracker should include scrapers for both providers (and others used in the organisation).

LLM vulnerability scanner. Run after every model upgrade to verify that the new model version does not introduce new injection susceptibilities. Results feed into the security review section of the YAML radar entry.
Exact version pin guarantees identical behaviour
Dated snapshot pin does not guarantee identical behaviour (infra changes)
Typically years; SemVer major version bump
Unit and integration tests catch regressions
Prompt regression tests needed; semantic equivalence is harder to assert
Must test prompt compatibility and SDK compatibility independently
Feature flag flips traffic back to previous model — no code deploy
Model upgrades may significantly change per-token cost
Security review + compliance check + behaviour assessment
Model version in the threat model:

When a provider patches a security issue in a model (e.g., improved injection resistance), the fix is only available in the new model version. Organisations that have not upgraded are still exposed. Monitor provider security advisories and treat model upgrades that include security fixes as higher priority than routine capability upgrades.

Verify the SHA-256 hash of model weights against the provider's published checksum before loading them into any inference environment. A compromised weights file (backdoored model) is the AI supply chain equivalent of a compromised package.

Log the model version ID alongside every agent action in the audit trail. If a model version is later found to have a security vulnerability, the audit trail enables forensic analysis of which sessions were exposed and what data they processed.

The lifecycle tracker and dashboard are operational tools that the team depends on to manage migration timelines. Run them with high availability and alert on tracker failures independently of the model monitoring they perform.
